Polish Semester 2

Stomach

Review and Deepen Your Understanding of the Chapter's Core Concepts...

Section

Jump to Segment

01Stomach 02Mucosa 03Mucous Neck Cells 04Parietal Cells 05Chief Cells 06Enteroendocrine Cells 07Other Layers
01

Stomach

▾

General Functions of the Stomach

  • Carbohydrate Digestion: Continues the initial breakdown of carbohydrates started by salivary amylase.
  • Acidification & Mixing: Adds acidic fluid to ingested food. Mixes contents into a viscous mass called chyme via the churning activity of the muscularis externa.
  • Lipid Digestion: Promotes initial digestion of triglycerides via enzyme lipase.
  • Protein Digestion: Promotes initial digestion of proteins via enzyme pepsin.

Major Anatomical Regions & Their Histological Roles

Cardia

  • Narrow transitional zone (1.5–3 cm wide).
  • Immediately surrounding the esophageal opening.
  • Primary Function: Mucus production.

Fundus & Body

  • Comprise the much larger, proximal regions.
  • Identical to each other histologically.
  • Primary Function: Sites of gastric glands responsible for releasing acidic gastric juice.

Pylorus

  • Funnel-shaped distal region opening into the small intestine.
  • Primary Function: Mucus production (histologically similar to cardia).

Mucosal Topography & Common Wall Structure

Rugae

  • Large, longitudinally directed folds of the mucosa and submucosa.
  • Present when the stomach is empty.
  • Flatten and disappear as the stomach fills with food.

Wall Layers

  • All four major layers of the GI tract are present and well-defined in all stomach regions:
    • Mucosa
    • Submucosa
    • Muscularis
    • Serosa
02

Mucosa

▾

Epithelium & Gastric Pits

  • Epithelium changes abruptly at the esophagogastric junction (from stratified squamous to simple columnar).
  • Epithelium Type: Simple Columnar Epithelium.
  • Epithelium invaginates deeply into the lamina propria, forming millions of gastric pits.
  • Each gastric pit has an opening to the stomach lumen.

Surface Mucous Cells

  • Line the entire luminal surface and the gastric pits.
  • Secrete thick, adherent, highly viscous mucous layer rich in bicarbonate ions.
  • Protects mucosa from abrasive food particles and corrosive stomach acid.

Lamina Propria & Gastric Glands

  • Gastric pits lead into long, branched, tubular gastric glands.
  • Glands extend through the full thickness of the lamina propria, from the base of the pits to the muscularis mucosae.

Lamina Propria

  • Highly vascularized.
  • Contains:
    • Smooth muscle fibers
    • Lymphoid cells
    • Capillaries
    • Lymphatics
  • Surrounds and supports gastric pits and glands.

Stem Cells & Epithelial Renewal (Isthmus)

  • Location of stem cells: Isthmus / Neck (narrow segment connecting the gastric pit to the gastric gland).
  • Stem Cell Type: Pluripotent stem cells — divide asymmetrically.
  • Produce progenitor cells for all other epithelial cells.

Upward Migration

  • Differentiate to replace surface mucous cells.
  • Surface cell turnover time: 4–7 days.

Downward Migration

  • Differentiate to replace secretory cells of the gastric glands.
  • Glandular cell turnover time: Much slower than surface mucous cells.

Muscularis Mucosae

  • Layer of smooth muscle separating the mucosa from the underlying submucosa.

Regional Specificity: Fundus & Body

  • In the fundus and body, gastric glands fill most of the mucosa.
  • Several gastric glands branch from the isthmus (neck) of each gastric pit.

Secretory Epithelium Distribution

  • Secretory epithelial cells of the gastric glands are distributed unevenly and release products that are key to the stomach's functions.
  • Four Major Cell Types:
    • Mucous Neck Cells
    • Parietal (Oxyntic) Cells
    • Chief (Zymogenic) Cells
    • Enteroendocrine Cells (DNES Cells)
03

Mucous Neck Cells

▾

Location & General Morphology

Location

  • Primarily in the neck region of gastric glands.
  • Occur in clusters but also singly among other cell types.

Cell Population

  • Progenitor cells.
  • Immature surface mucous cells.

Morphology

  • Less columnar (shorter) than surface mucous cells.
  • Shape often distorted by neighboring cells.
  • Round nucleus.
  • Apical secretory granules.

Mucus Secretion

  • Less alkaline than the mucous secreted by surface mucous cells.
04

Parietal (Oxyntic) Cells

▾

Location & General Morphology

Location

  • Among mucous neck cells.
  • Throughout deeper parts of gastric glands.

Morphology

  • Large cells.
  • Rounded or pyramidal shape.
  • One (sometimes two) central round nucleus.

Cytoplasmic Staining

  • Intensely eosinophilic due to high density of mitochondria.

Ultrastructure: Active vs Resting State

Active Parietal Cell (Stimulated)

  • Deep, circular invagination of apical plasma membrane forms large intracellular canaliculi via tubular vesicles fusing with the cell membrane.
  • Canaliculus is lined by thousands of microvilli — greatly increased surface area for diffusion and ion pumps.
  • Contains mitochondria and Golgi vesicles.
  • Prolonged activity may produce more mitochondria.

Resting Parietal Cell (Unstimulated)

  • Contains numerous tubular vesicles below the apical cell membrane.
  • Few microvilli.
  • Short intracellular canaliculi.
  • Contains mitochondria and Golgi vesicles.

HCl Synthesis Mechanism

  • 1. Water Splitting: Water (H₂O) within the parietal cells splits into H⁺ and OH⁻.
  • 2. Proton Pump (Apical Side): H⁺ is pumped into the lumen of the gastric gland via H⁺/K⁺ ATPase (apical membrane).
  • 3. Bicarbonate Formation: OH⁻ combines with CO₂ (catalyzed by carbonic anhydrase) to form HCO₃⁻ (bicarbonate ion).
  • 4. Anion Exchange (Basal Side): HCO₃⁻ is transported out of the parietal cell into the blood/interstitial fluid via a Cl⁻/HCO₃⁻ antiport. Cl⁻ is transported into the parietal cell.
  • 5. Chloride Exit: Cl⁻ diffuses through channels into the intracellular canaliculus.
  • 6. HCl Formation: H⁺ and Cl⁻ combine to form Hydrochloric Acid (HCl) in the lumen of the gastric gland.

Bicarbonate Fate & Mucosal Protection

Basal Release

  • HCO₃⁻ enters interstitial fluid and microvasculature, helping maintain a neutral pH in the mucosa (despite highly acidic gastric secretion).

Mucous Alkalization

  • Other HCO₃⁻ is taken up by surface mucous cells and used to raise the pH of the protective mucus layer.

Mitochondria

  • Abundant mitochondria present to provide energy (ATP) for operating the cells' ion pumps (H⁺/K⁺ ATPase).

Additional Secretion: Intrinsic Factor

  • Secretion of intrinsic factor (glycoprotein) is required for uptake of Vitamin B12 in the small intestine (ileum).

Regulation of Secretory Activity

Parietal cell secretion is stimulated by:

  • Parasympathetic Innervation: Vagus nerve (neurotransmitter: acetylcholine).
  • Paracrine Release: Histamine (from local mast / enterochromaffin cells).
  • Polypeptide Hormone: Gastrin (from enteroendocrine G cells in pylorus).
05

Chief (Zymogenic) Cells

▾

Location & General Morphology

Location

  • Predominate in the lower (basal) regions of the gastric glands, near the muscularis mucosae.
  • Found in clusters.

Distribution Trend

  • Bases of glands contain fewer parietal cells but many more chief cells.

Morphology

  • Basal nucleus.
  • Abundant RER.
  • Numerous apical secretory granules.

Staining

  • Cytoplasmic staining is basophilic due to the abundant RER for protein synthesis.
  • Zymogen granules are often removed or stain poorly in routine histological preparations.

Secretory Products

Pepsinogens

  • Inactive zymogen precursors (proenzymes).
  • Converted into active pepsins in the acidic environment of the stomach lumen.

Pepsin Characteristics

  • Endoproteinases (cleave internal peptide bonds).
    • Broad specificity.
    • Optimal pH: Between 1.8 – 3.5 (highly acidic).

Pepsin Function

  • Initiate the hydrolysis of ingested proteins in the stomach.

Gastric Lipase

  • Function: Digests many lipids — begins initial digestion of triglycerides in the stomach.
06

Enteroendocrine Cells (Stomach)

▾

General Features

  • Scattered epithelial cells in the gastric and intestinal mucosa.
  • Function: Endocrine (systemic) or Paracrine (local) effects.

Visualization

  • Routine Light Microscopy: Rarely seen clearly.
  • Specialized Stains (Historical):
    • Chromium salts → Enterochromaffin (EC) cells.
    • Silver salts → Argentaffin cells.
  • Modern Method: Immunohistochemistry using antibodies against specific hormone products.

Nomenclature

  • Named with the initial letter of the main hormone produced (e.g. G cell for Gastrin).

Classification Systems

  • APUD cells: "Amine Precursor Uptake and Decarboxylation" (most enteroendocrine cells process amines).
  • DNES cells: Diffuse Neuroendocrine System (broader classification of APUD cells).

Morphological Types (Two Classes)

Closed Type

  • Broad base of cell lies on basal lamina — do not contact glandular lumen as they lack apical microvilli.
  • Cellular apex is covered by neighboring epithelial cells.
  • Secretion Mode: Release hormones basally in response to indirect signaling — neural, immune or paracrine factors.

Open Type

  • Broad base of cell lies on basal lamina — contain apical microvilli that contact the glandular lumen.
  • Apical microvilli bear chemoreceptors that sample the luminal contents.
  • Secretion Mode: Act as primary chemosensors for appetite and digestion, releasing hormones basally in response to direct luminal stimuli (e.g. pH, nutrients).

Regional Distribution in Stomach

Fundus

  • Enteroendocrine cells located at the basal lamina of the gastric glands — CLOSED TYPE.
  • Cell Type & Product: Enterochromaffin (EC) / Argentaffin cells → Serotonin (5-hydroxytryptamine).

Pylorus

  • Enteroendocrine cells located in contact with the glandular lumens — OPEN TYPE.
  • Cell Type & Product: G cells → Gastrin.

Major Hormone Effects

  • Regulation of peristalsis & gut motility.
  • Secretion of digestive enzymes, water & electrolytes.
  • Sense of satiety after eating (e.g. GLP-1 inhibits hunger).

Cardiac Glands & Pyloric Glands

Location

  • Found in the cardia and pylorus regions of the stomach.

Structural Features

  • Tubular glands.
  • Long gastric pits.
  • Branch into coiled secretory portions.

Cellular Composition & Secretions

  • Lack both parietal cells and chief cells (key distinction from fundus/body glands).
  • Few enteroendocrine G cells are present (particularly in the pylorus).
  • Primarily secreting mucous.

Principal Enteroendocrine Cells in the GI Tract

CellLocationHormoneMajor Action
D CellsPylorus, Duodenum, Pancreatic IsletsSomatostatinInhibitsSecretion from other DNES cells nearby
EC CellsStomach, Small & Large IntestinesSerotonin & Substance PPromotesIncreased gut motility
G CellsPylorusGastrinPromotesGastric acid secretion
I CellsSmall IntestineCholecystokinin (CCK)PromotesPancreatic enzyme secretion; Gallbladder contraction
InhibitsGastric acid secretion
K CellsDuodenum & JejunumGastric Inhibitory Polypeptide (GIP)InhibitsGastric acid secretion
L CellsIleum & ColonGLP-1 & Peptide YYPromotesInsulin secretion; H₂O & electrolyte absorption in large intestine
InhibitsGastric acid secretion; Sense of hunger
Mo CellsSmall IntestineMotilinPromotesIncreased gut motility
N CellsIleumNeurotensinInhibitsGastric acid secretion
S CellsSmall IntestineSecretinPromotesPancreatic & biliary bicarbonate and water secretion
InhibitsGastric acid secretion; Stomach emptying

Summary by Function

Inhibit Gastric Acid Secretion

  • D Cells (Somatostatin)
  • I Cells (CCK)
  • K Cells (GIP)
  • L Cells (GLP-1, Peptide YY)
  • N Cells (Neurotensin)
  • S Cells (Secretin)

Promote Gastric Acid Secretion

  • G Cells (Gastrin)

Promote Gut Motility

  • EC Cells (Serotonin, Substance P)
  • Mo Cells (Motilin)
07

Other Layers of Stomach

▾

Submucosa

  • Composition: Connective tissue.
  • Contents:
    • Large blood & lymph vessels.
    • Many lymphoid cells, macrophages & mast cells.
  • Present in all stomach regions.

Muscularis Externa

  • Three poorly defined layers of smooth muscle (unique compared to the typical two layers in most of the GI tract):
    • Outer Longitudinal Layer
    • Middle Circular Layer
    • Inner Oblique Layer
  • Function: Rhythmic contractions thoroughly mix ingested food and chyme with:
    • Mucus
    • HCl
    • Digestive enzymes

Pyloric Sphincter

  • Formed by a great thickening of the middle circular layer at the pylorus.
  • Controls release of chyme into the duodenum.

Serosa

  • Thin layer covering the exterior of the stomach.
